label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking for the writer's purpose in writing this passage. The options are A to D. Let me read the passage again to understand it better. The writer starts by saying they were 13 years old seven years ago (so 2001, when 9/11 happened). They describe being in a social studies class when a teacher announced a plane hit the WTC. They then talk about how the school day was cut short, and they watched TV both at school and at home. They mention walking to their Tae Kwon Do school and the impact of seeing the destroyed towers. The passage ends with the writer expressing that they'll never forget September 11th, even though they didn't lose loved ones but still felt the pain. Now, the options are about the purpose. Let's break down each option. A) To report an accident. Well, the writer is recounting a specific event, but it's from their personal perspective. Reporting an accident would be more factual, maybe like a news report. But this is more personal, so maybe not A. B) To warn people of the accident. The accident already happened, so warning people doesn't make sense. The writer is reflecting on past events, not trying to prevent a future one. So probably not B. C) To teach people a moral lesson. The passage doesn't seem to have a moral lesson per se. The writer is sharing their feelings and memories, not instructing others on a lesson to learn. So maybe not C. D) To share one of his life stories with others. This seems plausible. The writer is describing a significant event in their life, how they experienced it, their emotions, and the lasting
